schools.summary: The prohibition: 'my brethren, have not (do not hold) the faith of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Lord of glory, with respect of persons (prosopolempsiais — partiality, favouritism, judging by outward appearance)' — the profession of faith in Christ not to be joined with partiality toward persons (favouring the rich over the poor); 'the Lord of glory' (the glorious Lord, or the Lord who is the glory) a title of Christ's divine majesty, before whom earthly distinctions of wealth and rank are nothing, making partiality unfitting (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: partiality based on outward rank contradicts the faith of Christ, who came for all and esteemed not the outward; to honour the rich and slight the poor in the assembly betrays the gospel's spirit, which judges not by appearance.

themes: have not the faith of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Lord of glory, with respect of persons
the warning against holding the faith with partiality
favouritism incompatible with the gospel
the Lord of glory making earthly rank nothing

schools.summary: The supposed case: 'for if there come unto your assembly (synagogen — gathering, meeting) a man with a gold ring (chrysodaktylios — gold-ringed, a mark of wealth), in goodly apparel (splendid clothing), and there come in also a poor man in vile (shabby, dirty, mean) raiment' — James picturing two men entering the Christian assembly, one richly dressed (the gold ring and splendid clothing signalling a wealthy, distinguished visitor, the outward tokens of rank and means), one poor and shabby (outwardly unimpressive, lowly); the two set side by side to test how the assembly will treat them (Barnes, Robertson, Clarke).

themes: if there come unto your assembly a man with a gold ring, in goodly apparel
a poor man in vile raiment
the illustration of the rich and poor entering the assembly
the test of partiality

schools.summary: The partial treatment: 'and ye have respect to (look upon with favour, give special attention to) him that weareth the gay (splendid, fine) clothing, and say unto him, Sit thou here in a good place (an honourable seat); and say to the poor, Stand thou there, or sit here under my footstool (on the floor, at one's feet)' — the rich visitor shown deference and given the place of honour on account of his fine clothes, while the poor man is assigned a place of dishonour, made to stand or to sit on the ground; the contrast between the honour given the rich and the contempt shown the poor exposing the partiality, the assembly judging by outward appearance (Barnes, Robertson, JFB).

themes: ye have respect to him that weareth the gay clothing, and say... Sit thou here in a good place
say to the poor, Stand thou there, or sit here under my footstool
the partial welcome given to rich and poor
favouritism judging by appearance

schools.summary: The accusing question: 'are ye not then partial in yourselves (diekrithete — made distinctions among yourselves, wavered, were divided), and are become judges of evil thoughts (judges with evil reasonings, or judges who reason wickedly)?' — by such favouritism they have made unjust distinctions and become judges governed by evil motives; 'partial in yourselves' meaning they made a distinction, wavered, or were divided in their own minds, judging by the wrong standard (wealth) rather than by faith and worth; 'judges of evil thoughts' — in honouring the rich and slighting the poor they judge from corrupt motives, partiality corrupting judgment (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Are ye not then partial in yourselves, and are become judges of evil thoughts
the charge of being partial judges with evil thoughts
unjust distinctions from corrupt motives
partiality corrupting judgment

schools.summary: The rebuke of partiality by God's own pattern: 'hearken, my beloved brethren, Hath not God chosen the poor of this world (the poor in worldly goods) rich in faith (made spiritually rich though poor in goods), and heirs of the kingdom which he hath promised to them that love him?' — God himself having chosen many of the poor (the gospel received chiefly by the lowly), so that to despise the poor is to despise those God has chosen; though destitute in this world, they are rich in faith and heirs of God's kingdom (their true wealth spiritual), the kingdom promised to those who love God, love for God (not worldly wealth) marking the heirs (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: Hath not God chosen the poor of this world rich in faith, and heirs of the kingdom
which he hath promised to them that love him
God has chosen the poor to be rich in faith and heirs
despising the poor dishonours those God has honoured

schools.summary: The direct rebuke: 'but ye have despised (dishonoured, treated with contempt) the poor. Do not rich men oppress (exploit, tyrannize over, katadynasteuousin) you, and draw you before the judgment seats (drag you to court, to the tribunals)?' — in their partiality they had dishonoured the very people God had honoured (v.5), contradicting his choice; the pointed irony being that the very rich they flattered were often the ones who oppressed them and hauled the believers before courts (persecuting and prosecuting them), so it was foolish as well as wrong to honour the persecuting class who abused them (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: ye have despised the poor
Do not rich men oppress you, and draw you before the judgment seats
despising the poor while the rich oppress
flattering the persecuting class

schools.summary: The climax of the indictment of the rich: 'do not they blaspheme (revile, speak evil of) that worthy (honourable, excellent) name by the which ye are called (the name of Christ, named upon you, perhaps at baptism, the name that marks them as Christ's)?' — the rich oppressors not only persecuting the believers but blaspheming the very name of Christ by which they were called; so to flatter such blasphemers, while despising the poor who honour Christ, was doubly perverse (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Do not they blaspheme that worthy name by the which ye are called
the rich oppressors blaspheme Christ's worthy name
the name of Christ named upon the believers
flattering blasphemers doubly perverse

schools.summary: The supreme standard: 'if ye fulfil the royal law (basilikon — kingly, sovereign, supreme, the chief or king of laws) according to the scripture (as written, Leviticus 19:18), Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself, ye do well' — the law of love called 'royal' as supreme over all duties to others, the great commandment summing up duty to the neighbour; impartial love to all (rich and poor alike) fulfilling this royal law, partiality violating it; if they truly keep this law of impartial love they act rightly ('ye do well'), the test of their conduct toward rich and poor being whether it springs from genuine love to the neighbour, which knows no partiality (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: If ye fulfil the royal law according to the scripture, Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself, ye do well
the royal law of love to the neighbour
impartial love the supreme rule
love fulfilling the kingly law

schools.summary: The conclusion: 'but if ye have respect to persons (show partiality), ye commit sin (it is sin, a positive transgression), and are convinced (convicted, exposed) of (by) the law as transgressors' — partiality not a small fault but actual sin (a breach of the royal law of love, v.8, not to be excused as a trifle); the law that commands impartial love pronouncing them guilty, so that in showing partiality they break the law and stand condemned by it as transgressors (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: if ye have respect to persons, ye commit sin, and are convinced of the law as transgressors
partiality is sin, convicting them as transgressors
respect of persons a breach of the royal law
the law pronouncing them guilty

schools.summary: The principle: 'for whosoever shall keep the whole law, and yet offend (ptaise — stumble, fail) in one point (a single command), he is guilty of all' — the law a unity, so that to break one part is to become a transgressor of the law as a whole, guilty before it; 'guilty of all' meaning not that one who breaks one command is as guilty as if he had broken every command, but that he has become a transgressor of the law itself (he has broken the law, which is one, and so stands guilty before it); the law being the expression of one will of one Lawgiver (v.11), so that to violate any part is to set oneself against that will and break the whole — hence partiality, though 'one point,' makes one a transgressor (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: whosoever shall keep the whole law, and yet offend in one point, he is guilty of all
one offence makes one guilty of all
the law a unity, one breach making a transgressor
the seriousness of partiality

schools.summary: The ground of v.10: 'for he that said (the one Lawgiver, God), Do not commit adultery, said also, Do not kill. Now if thou commit no adultery, yet if thou kill, thou art become a transgressor of the law' — the same God having given every command, the authority behind each being one and the same; a man may keep one command (no adultery) yet break another (kill), but since the same God forbade both, breaking either defies that one authority, so that by breaking one command he has transgressed the law (as the expression of God's one will); the unity of the Lawgiver making any single breach a transgression of the whole (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: he that said, Do not commit adultery, said also, Do not kill
if thou kill, thou art become a transgressor of the law
the one Lawgiver makes any breach a transgression of the whole
the unity of the Lawgiver

schools.summary: The exhortation: 'so speak ye, and so do (let both word and deed be governed), as they that shall be judged by the law of liberty (the gospel law, the perfect law that frees, 1:25)' — living, in word and deed, as those who will be judged by the gospel's law of liberty; both speech and action to be regulated by the consciousness of coming judgment, the believer governing his whole conduct knowing he must answer for it; 'the law of liberty' being not the bondage of the old law but the gospel, which frees yet also judges, the rule by which the Christian's life is measured (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: So speak ye, and so do, as they that shall be judged by the law of liberty
the call to speak and act as those judged by the law of liberty
word and deed governed by coming judgment
the gospel the standard of judgment

schools.summary: The solemn rule: 'for he shall have judgment without mercy, that hath shewed no mercy' — the one who has shown no mercy to others (as in despising the poor) will himself be judged without mercy, God dealing with men as they deal with others (Matthew 5:7; 18:35); the unmerciful (those who show no compassion to the poor and needy) finding no mercy in their own judgment, mercilessness being a mark of an unrenewed heart that brings a merciless verdict; 'and mercy rejoiceth against (triumphs over, glories against) judgment' — for the merciful, mercy prevailing over judgment, the one who has shown mercy finding that mercy exults over the sentence (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Chrysostom: mercy triumphs over judgment — for those who have shown compassion, God's mercy exults over the sentence and prevails; mercilessness brings a merciless judgment, but the merciful are met with the mercy they showed, mercy glorying against the strict verdict of the law.

themes: he shall have judgment without mercy, that hath shewed no mercy
mercy rejoiceth against judgment
the merciless face judgment without mercy, but mercy triumphs
God dealing as men deal

schools.summary: The test case: 'what doth it profit (what good is it, what advantage), my brethren, though a man say (claims, professes) he hath faith, and have not works? can faith save him?' — the case being a mere profession of faith without the works that genuine faith produces (the emphasis on 'say,' a faith only claimed); such a workless, professed faith profiting nothing, unable to benefit the man who has only a faith of words. The precise sense of 'can faith (he pistis — THAT faith, the workless professed faith just described, with the article) save him?' is not whether faith saves, but whether this kind of faith (a barren profession) can save; the expected answer no — James not denying that true faith saves but denying that a faith without works (which is no true faith) can save (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: though a man say he hath faith, and have not works
can faith save him
the question whether a workless faith can save
a barren profession that is no saving faith

schools.summary: The example introduced to expose empty faith: 'if a brother or sister (a fellow-Christian, man or woman) be naked (gymnoi — poorly clad, ill-clothed, not wholly unclothed but lacking sufficient or proper clothing), and destitute (lacking) of daily food (the day's necessary sustenance)' — the case of a needy fellow-believer in genuine, pressing want of the basic necessities of life (clothing and food), the figure of real poverty and need (Barnes, Robertson, JFB).

themes: If a brother or sister be naked, and destitute of daily food
the case of a brother or sister in need
a fellow-believer in real want
ill-clad and lacking daily food

schools.summary: The empty response: 'and one of you say unto them, Depart in peace (a friendly dismissal), be ye warmed (clothed) and filled (fed); notwithstanding ye give them not those things which are needful (necessary) to the body; what doth it profit?' — fine words of good wishes, sending the needy away with a blessing but nothing more (warm words without warm help), the words belied by the empty hand that supplies nothing of what the body actually needs; such empty words profiting the needy nothing, and just so a faith of mere words without the works of love profiting nothing — the parallel exact: as words without deeds are useless to the poor, so faith without works is useless for salvation (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Depart in peace, be ye warmed and filled; notwithstanding ye give them not those things which are needful to the body
what doth it profit
the empty words that give no help
words without deeds, like faith without works

schools.summary: The application of the parable: 'even so faith, if it hath not works, is dead (nekra — lifeless, devoid of vitality), being alone (kath heauten — by itself, in itself)' — just as kind words without deeds are useless, so faith that produces no works is dead in itself, a lifeless thing; not merely weak but lifeless like a corpse, a faith bearing no fruit of works having no spiritual life (dead faith, not the living faith that saves); 'being alone' — faith standing by itself without works proving itself lifeless, works being the necessary expression of living faith (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Even so faith, if it hath not works, is dead, being alone
faith without works is dead
dead faith, not the living faith that saves
works the necessary expression of living faith

schools.summary: The imagined dialogue (a difficult verse): 'yea, a man may say, Thou hast faith, and I have works: shew me thy faith without thy works, and I will shew thee my faith by my works' — the sense being that one may distinguish the two (you claim faith, I point to works), the challenge testing whether faith can be shown apart from works; 'shew me thy faith without (apart from) thy works' the impossible demand (faith being invisible and only able to be evidenced by its fruits, so one cannot exhibit a workless faith); 'I will shew thee my faith by (ek — out of) my works' — works being the visible evidence of faith, the genuine believer proving his faith by his deeds, works making faith manifest (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: shew me thy faith without thy works, and I will shew thee my faith by my works
the challenge to show faith by works
faith invisible, evidenced only by its fruits
works the visible evidence of faith

schools.summary: The example of a merely intellectual faith: 'thou believest that there is one God (the fundamental creed, the unity of God); thou doest well (so far, good): the devils (demons) also believe (this same truth), and tremble (phrissousin — shudder, bristle with dread)' — to believe in one God right and good as far as it goes (orthodox belief commended), but the demons holding the same orthodox belief, so a bare assent to truth is something even demons possess (mere creedal faith no proof of saving faith); the demons' faith going beyond cold assent to terror, yet not saving or sanctifying them (producing dread, not love or obedience) — so a faith that is mere belief, even with feeling, without works, is no better than the demons' and cannot save (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Chrysostom: the devils believe and shudder — their faith is real assent, even with dread, yet it does not save them, for it produces only terror, not love or obedience; so the bare belief of which a man boasts, if it bears no fruit of works, is no better than the faith of demons.

themes: Thou believest that there is one God; thou doest well: the devils also believe, and tremble
even the devils believe and tremble
bare creedal faith no proof of saving faith
the demons' faith producing dread, not obedience

schools.summary: The pointed challenge: 'but wilt thou know (are you willing to learn, will you be convinced), O vain (kene — empty, foolish, senseless, empty-headed) man, that faith without works is dead (nekra — lifeless; some texts arge — barren, idle, useless)?' — James addressing the one who clings to a workless faith as 'vain' (a sharp but just rebuke of the empty, foolish, deceived man who imagines a workless faith can save) and pressing the central thesis (now to be proved by the examples of Abraham and Rahab) that faith divorced from works is lifeless and fruitless (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: wilt thou know, O vain man, that faith without works is dead
the appeal to the vain man that faith without works is dead
a sharp rebuke of the empty-headed
the thesis to be proved by example

schools.summary: Calvin and Gill: James does not speak of the cause or ground of justification (the manner in which men obtain righteousness) but of the proof or manifestation of righteousness by conduct, and that before men. 'Justified' here means shown, declared, demonstrated righteous: Abraham, justified before God by faith (Genesis 15:6), proved the reality of that faith by offering Isaac (Genesis 22), thirty years later. Calvin distinguishes the twofold meaning of 'justify' — Paul means the gratuitous imputation of righteousness before God's tribunal, James the manifestation of righteousness by works (as a chest of hidden riches is 'made known'). Gill: works are 'not as the causes of his justification... but as effects declaring it.' Calvin sharply rejects 'the Sophists' (the Roman Catholic reading) who from this verse 'cry out as being victorious, that justification is partly by works' — works conferring or meriting righteousness before God.

Barnes, Robertson, and Ellicott: James means 'justified' in the sense that a man's faith is shown to be genuine by works (the evidence, not the ground). Barnes: James does not affirm that good works are the ground of acceptance or make atonement, nor deny justification by faith (the very passage he quotes, Genesis 15:6, declares Abraham's faith was imputed for righteousness, an act long prior); he teaches that the faith which justifies is only that which leads to good works, the works proving it genuine. Robertson: James and Paul 'use the same words, but they are talking of different acts' — Paul of Abraham's faith as the basis of justification (Romans 4:1-5), James of the offering of Isaac as the fruit of faith Abraham already had; 'there is no contradiction at all between James and Paul. Neither is answering the other.'

Wesley and Clarke reconcile by the two justifications and two works: Paul speaks of justification by faith antecedent to works (the faith Abraham had at Genesis 15:6, many years before Isaac was born); James of justification 'made righteous' by works consequent to faith (the offering of Isaac). Wesley: 'St. James's justification by works is the fruit of St. Paul's justification by faith'; Abraham was justified in Paul's sense (accounted righteous) by faith, and in James's sense (made righteous, his faith perfected and proved) by works. Clarke: Abraham's works justified — proved the genuineness of — his faith; obedience to God is essential to maintain faith.

debates.question: How is James's 'justified by works' reconciled with Paul's 'justified by faith'?

sides.position: 'Justified' = shown/demonstrated/proved righteous; works are the evidence of a living faith, not the ground; no contradiction with Paul (the Protestant consensus)
Man is justified partly by works, works conferring or meriting righteousness before God (the Roman Catholic / 'Sophist' reading)
holders: the Roman Catholic interpreters Calvin names and rejects as 'the Sophists'

themes: Was not Abraham our father justified by works, when he had offered Isaac his son upon the altar
Abraham justified by works in offering Isaac
the great faith-and-works crux
works the evidence, not the ground, of justification

schools.summary: The relation of faith and works in Abraham: 'seest thou how faith wrought with (synergei — co-operated with, worked together with) his works, and by works was faith made perfect (eteleiothe — completed, brought to its full development)?' — Abraham's faith and works acting together, the faith the living principle that produced the obedient act of offering Isaac (faith the active root, not idle, working with the fruit, the energy behind the deed); 'made perfect' meaning not that faith was defective and remedied by works, but that it was carried out to its fair and full result, fully developed and expressed (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: faith was 'perfected by works' not because it received its perfection from them, but because it was thereby proved to be true and not idle; Abraham's faith was already formed and perfect before he sacrificed his son (Genesis 15:6), so the offering was not the perfecting of his faith in itself but the demonstration of its integrity — faith said to be perfected by works because it brought forth that remarkable fruit of obedience.

Wesley: faith and works have each their own energy; faith does not get its being from works (for it is before them) but its perfection — that vigour of faith which begets works is excited and increased by them, as the body's natural heat begets motion whereby itself is increased; works do not give life to faith, but faith begets works and is then perfected by them.

themes: Seest thou how faith wrought with his works, and by works was faith made perfect
faith wrought with works and was perfected by them
faith the living principle producing the deed
faith proved genuine and brought to full expression

schools.summary: The key text (Genesis 15:6): 'and the scripture was fulfilled (epleer othe — confirmed, carried out to its full expression) which saith, Abraham believed God, and it was imputed (counted, reckoned) unto him for righteousness: and he was called the Friend of God (2 Chronicles 20:7; Isaiah 41:8)' — the declaration that Abraham believed and was counted righteous (made long before Isaac's offering) confirmed and fulfilled by his obedient act, the offering proving the reality of the faith the Scripture records (the very passage Paul quotes in Romans 4:3 to show faith preceded and was the basis of justification); 'the Friend of God' a title of highest honour, the fruit of his faith and obedience (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin presses the chronology: the imputation of righteousness (Genesis 15:6) preceded the offering of Isaac by more than thirty years (faith was imputed fifteen years before Isaac was even born), so the imputation cannot be grounded on that work; James says the Scripture was 'fulfilled' (confirmed) by the later act, not that righteousness was then first imputed — proving that 'justified by works' means the manifestation, not the cause, of righteousness, 'something posterior' being pointed out. They who make the work the cause pervert Scripture, making the effect its own cause.

themes: the scripture was fulfilled which saith, Abraham believed God, and it was imputed unto him for righteousness
he was called the Friend of God
the Scripture fulfilled: Abraham believed and was the Friend of God
the imputation preceding the work by thirty years

schools.summary: Barnes and Robertson (with Ellicott): 'ye see then how that by works a man is justified, and not by faith only' — 'justified' meaning shown, declared, proved righteous, not justified before God by the merit of works; works being the evidence by which a man's faith is shown genuine and he is seen to be justified. Barnes: justification takes place in God's sight by faith, but it is only the faith that produces good works that justifies, so good works (as the expression of faith) are necessary, and James contradicts a false explanation of Paul, not Paul himself. Robertson: 'is justified' here is 'not made righteous, but shown to be righteous.' Ellicott: James puts 'only' emphatically last — he never denies justification by faith, but that fancied justification by an idle, speculative faith with no acts of love.

Wesley: Paul ('a man is justified by faith,' not by works, Romans 3:28) and James do not contradict, because they do not speak of the same faith (Paul of living faith, James of dead faith) nor of the same works (Paul of works antecedent to faith, James of works subsequent to it); so 'not by faith only' condemns the dead, workless faith, not the living faith that justifies and necessarily produces works.

Gill: 'not as causes procuring justification, but as effects declaring it' — the best works are imperfect and cannot be a righteousness justifying before God; justification in that sense would frustrate grace, make void Christ's death, and encourage boasting; good works do not go before justification as causes or conditions but follow it as fruits and effects. The same confessional edge as v.21 stands: the Protestant consensus holds works the evidence, the Roman Catholic reading (rejected) takes the verse to teach justification partly by the merit of works.

debates.question: Does 'not by faith only' teach that works share in justifying before God, or that the justifying faith is one that works?

sides.position: The justifying faith is a working faith; works are the evidence (not the meritorious ground) by which a man is shown righteous; no contradiction with Paul (the Protestant consensus)
Man is justified partly by works, works meriting/contributing to righteousness before God (the Roman Catholic reading, rejected by the Reformers)
holders: the Roman Catholic interpreters the Reformers oppose

themes: Ye see then how that by works a man is justified, and not by faith only
justified by works, and not by faith only
the faith-and-works crux restated
the justifying faith is a working faith

schools.summary: The second example (in deliberate contrast with Abraham): 'likewise also was not Rahab the harlot justified by works, when she had received (welcomed, sheltered) the messengers (the Israelite spies, Joshua 2), and had sent them out another way (helped them escape)?' — Rahab, as different from Abraham as possible (a Gentile, a woman, a harlot), the second proof, also 'justified by works' in the same sense (shown to be righteous, her faith proved genuine by her act of receiving the spies and aiding their escape); James joining the most eminent patriarch and a harlot alien to show that no one of any condition is counted righteous without good works, whosoever would be counted righteous (even the lowest) must show it by works (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: was not Rahab the harlot justified by works, when she had received the messengers, and had sent them out another way
Rahab also justified by works, proving faith genuine
patriarch and harlot, none justified without works
her deed demonstrating faith

schools.summary: The apt closing figure: 'for as the body without the spirit (pneuma — breath, the animating principle, the life) is dead (a lifeless corpse), so faith without works is dead also' — the body, when the spirit (life-breath) has departed, a dead carcass, the comparison drawing on the most obvious mark of death; faith without works as lifeless as a corpse, works being to faith what the breath/life is to the body (the evidence and accompaniment of life). Calvin cautions the figure does not make works the life of faith (that would reverse the truth, for faith is the root) but means that faith having no works is like a dead carcass without life — the whole argument summed up: living faith works, dead faith does not (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: as the body without the spirit is dead, so faith without works is dead also
faith without works is dead as a body without the spirit
works the evidence of life, as breath to the body
living faith works, dead faith does not
